lists.title: Turkish A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in the order of the standard CEFR-aligned Turkish curricula (Yedi İklim / İstanbul): 'to be' suffixes and demonstratives first, then plural, questions, existence, possession, cases, and the present continuous.

lists.title: Turkish A2 Grammar Path
lists.description: The core suffix machine: witnessed past, future, dative/ablative/genitive, the aorist, ability, comparison, postpositions.

lists.title: Turkish B1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Nuance and combination: evidential -miş, necessity, conditionals, verbal nouns, gibi/kadar, -ken.

lists.title: Turkish B2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Complex sentences the Turkish way: -en/-dik participles, passive, causative, reported speech, converbs.

points.title: Personal pronouns and 'to be' (-(y)im, -sin…)
points.explanation: Turkish has no separate verb 'to be' in the present — a personal suffix attaches straight to the noun or adjective: öğrenci-yim (I am a student), öğretmen-sin (you are a teacher), o doktor (he/she is a doctor — third person takes no suffix). The suffix vowel follows vowel harmony, and -y- is inserted after a vowel: iyi-yim (I'm fine).
points.culture_note: Dropping the pronoun is normal — 'Öğrenciyim' alone is a complete, natural sentence. The pronoun is added for contrast or emphasis.

references.title: Personal endings and 'to be'
references.book: Göksel & Kerslake, Turkish: A Comprehensive Grammar (Routledge)

related.title: Var / yok — there is, there isn't
related.contrast: Var says something EXISTS; the -im endings say what something IS.

related.title: Question particle (mı / mi / mu / mü)
related.contrast: The personal ending hops onto mı in questions: öğretmen misin?

points.title: Demonstratives (bu, şu, o)
points.explanation: Three distances: bu (this, near me), şu (that, a bit away / something I'm pointing at), o (that, over there — also 'he/she/it'). With a noun they work like English: bu kitap (this book), o ev (that house).

related.title: Personal pronouns and 'to be' (-(y)im, -sin…)
related.contrast: O is both 'that' and 'he/she/it' — context decides.

related.title: Plural (-ler / -lar)
related.contrast: Demonstratives stay singular before plural nouns: bu evler.

points.title: Plural (-ler / -lar)
points.explanation: Form the plural by adding -ler after front vowels and -lar after back vowels. Important exception: after a number, the noun stays singular — üç kitap ('three books'), never üç kitaplar.

drills.translation: There are two cats in the house.
drills.hint: noun stays SINGULAR after a number

drills.translation: The children are in the garden.
The books are on the table.
There are three trees in the garden.
The cats are in the park.

related.title: Possessive suffixes (-im, -in, -i…)
related.contrast: Plural comes BEFORE possession: ev-ler-im, my houses.

related.title: Demonstratives (bu, şu, o)
related.contrast: Bu evler, not bunlar evler — the noun carries the plural.

points.title: Question particle (mı / mi / mu / mü)
points.function: Turn any statement into a yes/no question
points.explanation: Add the particle after the word you are asking about; it is written separately and harmonizes with the last vowel: kitap mı?, öğrenci mi?, doktor mu?, Türk mü? Personal endings attach to the particle: Öğrenci misin? (Are you a student?).

drills.hint: question particle — harmonize with the last vowel
question particle — harmonize with the last vowel
question particle — harmonize with the last vowel
question particle — harmonize with the last vowel
question particle — harmonize with the last vowel

related.title: Negative present continuous (-miyor)
related.contrast: -miyor negates, mı asks — and they stack: gelmiyor mu?

related.title: Present continuous (-iyor)
related.contrast: Geliyor musun? — the particle splits off the -iyor verb.

points.title: Locative case (-de / -da)
points.explanation: The locative suffix means 'in/on/at'. It harmonizes with the last vowel of the word: -de after front vowels (e, i, ö, ü) and -da after back vowels (a, ı, o, u). After a voiceless consonant it hardens to -te/-ta.
points.culture_note: Vowel harmony runs through almost all Turkish suffixes — learning to hear front vs back vowels pays off everywhere.

drills.translation: The book is on the table.
The key is in the bag.
The children are in the garden.
The milk is in the fridge.

related.title: Accusative case (-ı / -i / -u / -ü)
related.contrast: Locative marks WHERE; accusative marks WHAT the action lands on.

related.title: Var / yok — there is, there isn't
related.contrast: Locative + var = 'there is X at Y': masada kitap var.

points.title: Var / yok — there is, there isn't
points.function: Say what exists and what doesn't
points.explanation: 'Var' asserts existence and 'yok' denies it; both come at the end: Evde süt var (There is milk at home), Parkta kimse yok (There is nobody in the park). With the locative this is also how Turkish says 'have': Bende para var (I have money, lit. 'at me money exists').

drills.translation: There is no one in the park.
There is no money in the bag.
There is a tree in the garden.

related.title: Locative case (-de / -da)
related.contrast: Existence statements usually open with a locative: evde kimse yok.

related.title: Negative present continuous (-miyor)
related.contrast: Yok negates existence; -miyor negates actions.

points.title: Possessive suffixes (-im, -in, -i…)
points.explanation: Possession is marked ON the possessed noun: ev-im (my house), ev-in (your house), ev-i (his/her house). The optional genitive pronoun adds emphasis: benim evim. Vowels harmonize: araba-m (my car), araban (your car).

related.title: Plural (-ler / -lar)
related.contrast: Order is fixed: plural, then possessive — evlerim.

related.title: Accusative case (-ı / -i / -u / -ü)
related.contrast: A possessed object still takes accusative: evimi görüyorum.

points.title: Present continuous (-iyor)
points.function: Talk about what is happening now
points.explanation: The -iyor suffix marks an ongoing action ('am/is/are doing'). Its buffer vowel harmonizes (ı/i/u/ü) and a personal ending follows. For 'I', add -um: oku → okuyorum, iç → içiyorum.

drills.translation: They are playing in the park.

related.title: Negative present continuous (-miyor)
related.contrast: Same tense, negated: -m(a/e) squeezes in before -yor.

related.title: Question particle (mı / mi / mu / mü)
related.contrast: Geliyor mu? — questions add the particle after -iyor.

points.title: Negative present continuous (-miyor)
points.explanation: Insert the negative -m(a/e)- before -iyor; the vowel harmonizes and shrinks: iç-m-iyor-um → içmiyorum (I am not drinking), izle-m-iyor → izlemiyor (he isn't watching). The stress falls just before the negative.

drills.hint: to drink + negative present continuous, I
to watch + negative present continuous, he/she
to eat + negative present continuous, we

drills.translation: You are not listening to me.
drills.hint: to listen + negative present continuous, you
to work + negative present continuous, they
to sleep + negative present continuous, he/she

related.title: Present continuous (-iyor)
related.contrast: The affirmative twin — drop the -m- and it happens again.

related.title: Var / yok — there is, there isn't
related.contrast: -miyor is 'isn't doing'; yok is 'there isn't any'.

points.title: Accusative case (-ı / -i / -u / -ü)
points.explanation: A DEFINITE direct object takes the accusative: Kitabı okuyorum (I am reading THE book) vs Kitap okuyorum (I read books / a book, indefinite — no suffix). The vowel harmonizes four ways, and final p/ç/t/k soften: kitap → kitabı.
points.culture_note: Whether the object is 'definite' decides the suffix — a meaning-driven case system rather than a mechanical one.

drills.translation: The child is drinking the milk.

related.title: Locative case (-de / -da)
related.contrast: Accusative = what you act on; locative = where it happens.

related.title: Possessive suffixes (-im, -in, -i…)
related.contrast: After a possessive the accusative shows as -ni/-nı: arabasını.

points.title: Past tense (-di)
points.explanation: The seen/witnessed past adds -di with four-way vowel harmony and consonant hardening after voiceless sounds (-ti): gitmek → gittim (I went), gelmek → geldi (she came), yapmak → yaptık (we did). Person endings follow: -m, -n, —, -k, -niz, -ler.

drills.translation: Yesterday we went to the cinema.
I drank coffee in the morning.

points.title: Future tense (-ecek)
points.explanation: -ecek/-acak marks the future; before a vowel ending the k softens to ğ: aramak → arayacağım (I will call), gelmek → gelecekler (they will come). The buffer -y- appears after vowel stems.

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will work a lot.
Next week we will go to Istanbul.

drills.hint: to come + future; the question particle stays separate

points.title: Dative case (-e / -a)
points.explanation: The dative marks direction ('to'): ev → eve (to home), okul → okula (to school). A buffer -y- appears after vowels: araba → arabaya. Verbs of motion and giving take it: Okula gidiyorum, Anneme verdim.

drills.translation: In the evening I return home.
Tomorrow we are going to Istanbul.
The child is running to the park.
I bought this present for you.
I gave the book to my friend.

points.title: Ablative case (-den / -dan)
points.explanation: The ablative marks source ('from'): iş → işten (from work; voiceless ş hardens d→t), okul → okuldan (from school). It also builds comparisons and material: taştan (of stone).

points.title: Genitive + possessed (-in … -i)
points.explanation: Ownership marks BOTH nouns: the owner takes genitive -(n)in, the owned takes the possessive: öğretmenin evi (the teacher's house), arabanın kapısı (the car's door, with buffer -s- after a vowel).

drills.translation: The door of the house is open.
My mother's cooking is very good.
The colour of the sea is blue.

points.title: Aorist / geniş zaman (-ir)
points.explanation: The aorist covers habits, general truths, and offers: içmek → içerim (I drink [habitually]), kalkmak → kalkar (he gets up). Single-syllable stems mostly take -ar/-er; longer stems take -ir/-ır/-ur/-ür.
points.culture_note: The aorist is also the politeness tense: 'Bir çay alır mısınız?' (Would you take a tea?).

drills.translation: He reads a book every evening.
On Sundays we get up late.

points.title: Ability (-ebil)
points.explanation: -(y)ebil/-(y)abil expresses ability and permission: konuşmak → konuşabilirim (I can speak), gelmek → gelebilir (he can come). It conjugates like an aorist afterward.

drills.hint: to come + ability (+ separate question particle)

points.title: Comparatives (daha / en)
points.explanation: Comparison is analytic: daha + adjective = more (Bu ev daha büyük), en + adjective = most (en iyi öğrenci). 'Than' is the ablative: senden daha uzun (taller than you).

drills.translation: He is the best student in the class.
Coffee is more expensive than tea.
This is the coldest day of the year.

points.title: Postpositions ile and için
points.function: Say with what and for whom
points.explanation: ile = with/by (arkadaşım ile / arkadaşımla — it often fuses as -le/-la); için = for (senin için, bu iş için). Pronouns take genitive before için: benim için (for me).

drills.translation: I went to the cinema with my friend.
I would do anything for you.

points.title: Before and after (-den önce / sonra)
points.explanation: önce (before) and sonra (after) follow an ablative noun: kahvaltıdan sonra (after breakfast), dersten önce (before class). With verbs: -meden önce (before doing), -dikten sonra (after doing).

drills.translation: After breakfast I take a walk.
Before sleeping I read a book.
Wash your hands before the meal.
After the lesson I went home.
Before the exam I studied a lot.

points.title: Aorist negative (-mez / -mem)
points.explanation: The aorist negates irregularly: -mem (I), -mezsin, -mez, -meyiz, -mezsiniz, -mezler: et yemem (I don't eat meat), yalan söylemez (he doesn't lie).

drills.translation: I don't drink coffee in the mornings.

points.title: Evidential past (-miş)
points.explanation: -miş marks hearsay, inference, and surprise — the un-witnessed past: Dün yağmur yağmış (It apparently rained yesterday), Çok yorulmuşsun (You must be really tired). Turkish forces you to say HOW you know.
points.culture_note: This -di/-miş split is one of Turkish's signatures: saying 'geldi' claims you saw it; 'gelmiş' says you found out.

drills.hint: to get tired + evidential past, you (inference)

drills.translation: Apparently you worked a lot yesterday.
The neighbours have apparently moved away.
The baby has apparently fallen asleep.
The food has turned out great (they say).

points.title: Necessity (-meli)
points.explanation: -meli/-malı expresses obligation: kalkmalıyım (I must get up), çalışmalısın (you should study), gitmeliyiz (we must go).

drills.translation: We should go to bed earlier.
drills.hint: to go to bed + necessity, we

drills.translation: She should go to the doctor.

points.title: Conditional (-se)
points.explanation: -se/-sa on the verb (or var/yok) builds real conditions: Zamanın varsa, gel (If you have time, come), Yağmur yağarsa evde kalırız (If it rains we stay home — aorist + -sa).

drills.translation: If it rains, we will stay at home.
If I have money, I'll buy a car.
If we get up early, we'll have breakfast.
drills.hint: to get up + aorist + conditional, we

drills.translation: If you want, we'll work together.
If I'm late, don't wait for me.

points.title: Verbal nouns (-mek / -me)
points.explanation: Infinitives act as nouns: Kitap okumak güzeldir (Reading books is nice). As an object the -me form takes case: Yüzmeyi seviyorum (I love swimming — yüzme + accusative).

drills.translation: Swimming is good for your health.

points.title: gibi and kadar
points.explanation: gibi = like (Aslan gibi güçlü — strong like a lion); kadar = as much as (Senin kadar hızlı değilim — I'm not as fast as you; pronouns take genitive before kadar).

drills.translation: I am not as fast as you.
My brother is not as tall as me.
He is as hardworking as his father.

points.title: While (-ken)
points.explanation: -ken attaches to the aorist (or nouns) for 'while': Çalışırken müzik dinlerim (While working I listen to music), Yemek yerken konuşma (Don't talk while eating). It ignores vowel harmony — always -ken.

drills.translation: While working, I listen to music.
The phone rang while I was sleeping.
I fell asleep while reading a book.
Don't look at your phone while studying.

points.title: Subject participle (-en)
points.explanation: -en/-an turns a verb into 'the one who…': gelen adam (the man who is coming), kitap okuyan kız (the girl reading a book). It replaces English 'who/that' clauses where the head noun is the subject.

drills.translation: The man who is coming is my father.
The girl who is reading a book is my sister.
The woman who is speaking is my mother.
The children playing in the garden are happy.
The students who studied passed the exam.
I saw the man running in the street.

points.title: Object participle (-dik)
points.function: Describe the thing that is done
points.explanation: When the head noun is the OBJECT, use -dik + possessive: okuduğum kitap (the book that I read), senin yaptığın yemek (the food you made). The possessive shows who did it; k softens to ğ before vowels.

drills.translation: The book that I read is very good.
The food that you made was delicious.
The film we watched was very long.
The city you live in is apparently very beautiful.
The book I am reading is on the table.

points.title: Passive voice (-il / -in)
points.function: Say what is done without the doer
points.explanation: The passive inserts -il (after consonants), -in (after l), or -n (after vowels): yapmak → yapıldı (it was built), içmek → içilir (it is drunk): Bu ev 1950'de yapıldı.

drills.translation: A lot of tea is drunk in Turkey.
This school was opened last year.
Turkish is spoken in this house.
The doors are closed at nine in the evening.

points.title: Causative (-dir / -t)
points.explanation: The causative makes someone do or have done: kesmek → kestirmek (have cut) — Saçımı kestirdim (I had my hair cut); yemek → yedirmek (feed): Annesi çocuğa yemek yedirdi.

drills.translation: The teacher had the students read a book.
I put the baby to sleep.

points.title: Reported speech (-diğini söylemek)
points.function: Report what people said and know
points.explanation: Reported clauses nominalize with -dik/-ecek + possessive + case: Geleceğini söyledi (He said he would come — gelecek+i+ni), Hasta olduğunu duydum (I heard that he was sick).

drills.translation: He said that he would come.
I heard that he was sick.
I didn't know that he was ill.

drills.hint: to live + object participle, my + accusative

drills.translation: She said that she loves me.
drills.hint: to love + object participle, her + accusative

drills.translation: I realised that I was late.
drills.hint: to be (late) + object participle, my + accusative

points.title: Converbs (-ip and -erek)
points.explanation: -ip links same-subject actions ('and then'): Kahvaltı yapıp evden çıktım (I had breakfast and left). -erek expresses manner ('by/while doing'): Koşarak geldi (He came running).

drills.translation: I had breakfast and left the house.
He closed the door and left.
I study while listening to music.

points.title: The more… (-dikçe)
points.function: Say 'the more X, the more Y'
points.explanation: -dikçe means 'as / the more / as long as': Okudukça öğreniyorum (The more I read, the more I learn), Sen güldükçe ben mutlu oluyorum (As you laugh, I become happy).

drills.translation: The more I read, the more I learn.
As you laugh, I become happy.
The more he earns, the more he spends.
You get more beautiful as you grow.
The more you learn Turkish, the easier it gets.

points.title: Without doing (-meden)
points.explanation: -meden = without doing: Veda etmeden gitti (He left without saying goodbye), Hiç çalışmadan sınavı geçti (He passed the exam without studying at all).

drills.translation: He passed the exam without studying at all.
He went to school without having breakfast.
He was walking without looking around.

points.title: Reflexive and reciprocal voice (-in / -iş)
points.function: Actions on oneself and each other
points.explanation: -in makes reflexives: yıkanmak (wash oneself) — Kedi yıkanıyor. -iş makes reciprocals: tanışmak (meet each other) — Biz üniversitede tanıştık (We met at university).

drills.translation: They see each other every day.
The friends met up after years.
I comb my hair every morning.
The two brothers are constantly fighting each other.

points.title: Because (-diği için)
points.explanation: -dik + possessive + için = because: Hasta olduğu için gelmedi (He didn't come because he was sick), Geç kaldığı için özür diledi (He apologized because he was late).

drills.translation: He didn't come because he was sick.
Because it rained, the match was cancelled.
He succeeded because he worked hard.
I am tired because I got up early.
drills.hint: to get up + object participle, my (+ 'because')

drills.translation: Because we didn't buy tickets, we couldn't get in.
drills.hint: to buy + negative + object participle, our (+ 'because')

points.title: Formal progressive (-mekte)
points.explanation: -mekte(dir) is the formal register's progressive, standard in news and reports: Toplantı devam etmektedir (The meeting is in progress), Fiyatlar artmaktadır (Prices are rising).

drills.translation: Work is ongoing on the project.

points.title: Pretending (-mış gibi)
points.explanation: Perfective participle + gibi = as if / pretending: Duymamış gibi yaptı (He pretended not to have heard), Hiçbir şey olmamış gibi davrandı (He acted as if nothing had happened).

drills.translation: He pretended not to have heard.
He acted as if nothing had happened.
He talks as if he doesn't know anything.
He recounts it as if he had seen everything.
She acted as if she didn't know me.

points.title: Instead of (-eceğine)
points.explanation: -eceğine = instead of (doing): Televizyon izleyeceğine kitap oku (Instead of watching TV, read a book), Şikayet edeceğine çözüm bul (Instead of complaining, find a solution).

drills.translation: Instead of watching TV, read a book.
Instead of complaining, find a solution.
Instead of sitting at home, go outside.
Instead of spending the money on games, buy books.
